title: Container OOM Kills
exclusions: Does not replace UC-3.1.1 wide exit-code taxonomy, UC-3.1.13 restart-policy back-off analytics, UC-3.1.22 HEALTHCHECK transitions, or UC-3.2.x Kubernetes pod resource quota narratives. Does not cover Windows containers with Hyper-V isolation, kernel-wide OOM killer decisions outside a container cgroup, pure JVM OutOfMemoryError exits that never breach cgroup limits, or swap-less host behavior when limits are disabled (memory.max set to max).
evidence: Saved search container_uc_3_1_2_oom_kills, lookups lookups/container_owner.csv and lookups/container_memory_baselines.csv, weekly CSV snapshots to the evidence index, dashboard row mirroring the SPL table projection, and git-tracked lookup versions attached to change records.

controlTest.positiveScenario: On a lab host run docker run --memory=64m --rm polinux/stress stress --vm 1 --vm-bytes 100M --timeout 30s, wait for collection, and confirm linux:cgroup shows an oom_kill counter delta, kern.log contains a Memory cgroup or oom-kill printk with pid and comm, docker:events records action=oom or a die with exitCode=137, and container_uc_3_1_2_oom_kills returns a critical row with non-zero oom_kill_count_15m and populated mem_limit_recommendation_mib.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Deploy nginx:alpine with docker run --memory=512m serving static content under normal load, verify memory.current stays well under memory.max without oom_kill increments, printk stays quiet, and the saved search produces no severity row for that container across several fifteen-minute runs.

dataSources: index=os sourcetype=linux:cgroup from Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (Splunkbase 833) scripted reads of cgroup-v2 control files: /sys/fs/cgroup/<scope>/memory.current, memory.max, memory.high, memory.events, memory.events.local, memory.swap.current, memory.swap.max, and memory.pressure PSI lines; cgroup-v1 fallback under /sys/fs/cgroup/memory/<docker cgroup>/memory.usage_in_bytes, memory.limit_in_bytes, memory.max_usage_in_bytes, memory.oom_control, and memory.failcnt. index=os sourcetype=syslog Universal Forwarder monitors of /var/log/kern.log and /var/log/syslog for kernel printk strings such as Memory cgroup out of memory: Killed process N (name) and oom-kill:constraint=...,task=...,pid=...,total-vm:...,rss:.... index=docker sourcetype=docker:events from Splunk Connect for Docker (Splunkbase 3303) for action=oom and correlated action=die with exitCode=137; optional sourcetype=docker:stats modular input for Engine memory.usage versus memory.limit time series. Alternative collection: Splunk OpenTelemetry Collector for Containers hostmetricsreceiver cgroup memory scrape exported through HEC per docs.splunk.com observability guidance.
app: Splunk Connect for Docker ([Splunkbase 3303](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/3303)) streams docker:events and optional docker:stats for Engine-level lifecycle and memory limit telemetry. Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux ([Splunkbase 833](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/833)) supplies linux:cgroup scripted reads and syslog ingestion for memory controller files and kernel printk correlation. Splunk OpenTelemetry Collector for Containers ([Splunk documentation](https://docs.splunk.com/observability/en/gdi/opentelemetry/install-k8s.html)) offers hostmetricsreceiver cgroup scraping when teams standardize on OpenTelemetry pipelines.
spl:
`comment("UC-3.1.2 Container OOM Kills. Tunables: indexes index=docker and index=os; lookups container_owner.csv (host lowercase, container_name, owner_team) and optional container_memory_baselines.csv for weekly seasonality; slope_bytes_per_min_warn=8388608; leak_pressure_pct_floor=80; sustained_pressure_pct=95. Owner field: owner_team. earliest=-1h@h latest=@h. UC-3.1.1 supplies the broader container death taxonomy so exit 137 is not confused with generic failures.")`
| multisearch
    [ search index=os sourcetype="linux:cgroup" earliest=-1h@h latest=@h
      | eval host=lower(toString(coalesce(host, Host, hostname, "")))
      | eval container_name=trim(toString(coalesce(container_name, containerName, docker_container, scope_task, "")))
      | eval oom_kill_counter=tonumber(tostring(coalesce(oom_kill, oom_kill_count, memory_events_oom_kill, memory_oom_kill_local, "")), 10)
      | where isnotnull(oom_kill_counter)
      | stats earliest(oom_kill_counter) AS oom_kill_first latest(oom_kill_counter) AS oom_kill_last BY host, container_name
      | eval oom_kill_count_15m=max(0, oom_kill_last - oom_kill_first)
      | eval arm="cgroup_counter" ]
    [ search index=os sourcetype=syslog earliest=-1h@h latest=@h (source="/var/log/kern.log" OR source="/var/log/syslog")
      | search (match(_raw, "(?i)Memory cgroup out of memory") OR match(_raw, "(?i)oom-kill:") OR match(_raw, "(?i)Killed process"))
      | eval host=lower(toString(coalesce(host, Host, hostname, "")))
      | rex field=_raw "(?i)Killed process (?<oom_killed_pid>\d+) \((?<oom_killed_comm>[^)]+)\)"
      | rex field=_raw "(?i)oom-kill:constraint=(?<oom_constraint>[^,]+),task=(?<oom_task>[^,]+),pid=(?<oom_killed_pid2>\d+)"
      | eval oom_killed_pid=coalesce(oom_killed_pid, oom_killed_pid2)
      | eval oom_killed_comm=coalesce(oom_killed_comm, oom_task, "")
      | stats latest(oom_killed_pid) AS oom_killed_pid latest(oom_killed_comm) AS oom_killed_comm count AS printk_burst BY host
      | eval arm="printk" ]
    [ search (index=os sourcetype="linux:cgroup") OR (index=docker sourcetype="docker:stats") earliest=-1h@h latest=@h
      | eval host=lower(toString(coalesce(host, Host, hostname, "")))
      | eval container_name=trim(toString(coalesce(container_name, containerName, name, "")))
      | eval memory_current_bytes=tonumber(tostring(coalesce(memory_current, memory_usage_in_bytes, memory_stats_usage, usage_mem, "")), 10)
      | eval memory_max_bytes=tonumber(tostring(coalesce(memory_max, memory_limit_in_bytes, memory_stats_limit, limit_mem, mem_limit, "")), 10)
      | eval image=toString(coalesce(Image, image, ""))
      | where isnotnull(memory_max_bytes) AND memory_max_bytes>0 AND isnotnull(memory_current_bytes)
      | eval memory_current_pct=round(100.0 * memory_current_bytes / memory_max_bytes, 2)
      | sort 0 + host, container_name, _time
      | streamstats window=12 current=t global=f first(memory_current_bytes) AS mc_first last(memory_current_bytes) AS mc_last first(_time) AS t0 last(_time) AS t1 BY host, container_name
      | eval span_minutes=if(t1>t0, (t1-t0)/60.0, 1.0)
      | eval slope_bytes_per_min=round((mc_last - mc_first) / span_minutes, 2)
      | stats max(memory_current_bytes) AS max_observed_rss_bytes max(memory_current_pct) AS memory_current_pct latest(memory_max_bytes) AS memory_max_bytes_latest max(slope_bytes_per_min) AS slope_bytes_per_min latest(image) AS image BY host, container_name
      | eval slope_mib_per_min=round(slope_bytes_per_min / 1024 / 1024, 3)
      | eval arm="pressure" ]
| eval merge_key=host."|".if(arm="printk","__host_printk__",container_name)
| stats max(oom_kill_count_15m) AS oom_kill_count_15m max(memory_current_pct) AS memory_current_pct max(max_observed_rss_bytes) AS max_observed_rss_bytes max(memory_max_bytes_latest) AS memory_max_bytes_latest max(slope_mib_per_min) AS slope_mib_per_min max(slope_bytes_per_min) AS slope_bytes_per_min latest(image) AS image latest(oom_killed_pid) AS oom_killed_pid latest(oom_killed_comm) AS oom_killed_comm max(printk_burst) AS printk_burst values(container_name) AS _cn BY host merge_key
| eval container_name=mvindex(_cn,0)
| eval container_name=if(isnull(container_name) OR len(container_name)==0, "unscoped_printk", container_name)
| eventstats max(memory_current_pct) AS pre_kill_pressure_hint BY host
| fillnull value=0 max_observed_rss_bytes memory_max_bytes_latest memory_current_pct slope_bytes_per_min oom_kill_count_15m printk_burst
| eval mem_limit_recommendation=round(max_observed_rss_bytes * 1.30 / 1024 / 1024, 0)
| eval mem_limit_recommendation_mib=mem_limit_recommendation
| eval memory_max_mib=if(memory_max_bytes_latest>0, round(memory_max_bytes_latest / 1024 / 1024, 1), null())
| eval memory_current_mib=if(max_observed_rss_bytes>0, round(max_observed_rss_bytes / 1024 / 1024, 1), null())
| eval severity=case(oom_kill_count_15m>0, "critical", memory_current_pct>95, "warning", (slope_bytes_per_min>8388608 AND memory_current_pct>80), "warning", true(), null())
| where isnotnull(severity) AND (NOT like(merge_key, "%|__host_printk__") OR oom_kill_count_15m>0 OR printk_burst>0)
| join type=left max=0 host, container_name
    [| inputlookup container_owner.csv
     | eval host=lower(toString(host))
     | eval container_name=toString(container_name)
     | eval owner_team=toString(coalesce(owner_team, squad, ""))
     | fields host container_name owner_team ]
| table host container_name image oom_kill_count_15m memory_current_pct memory_current_mib memory_max_mib mem_limit_recommendation_mib oom_killed_pid oom_killed_comm slope_mib_per_min severity owner_team
description: Detects cgroup memory controller oom_kill counter increments from linux:cgroup samples, sustained memory.current ratios approaching memory.max including breaching ninety percent, rising working-set slopes derived from rolling memory.current deltas, and kernel printk victims named in Memory cgroup out of memory or oom-kill lines with pid and comm extracted for cgroup-local attribution. The control correlates those kernel-side signals with optional docker:stats pressure and ties results to UC-3.1.1 so exit code 137 deaths are interpreted as out-of-memory kills rather than unrelated signals. It does not substitute restart-policy delay analytics (UC-3.1.13), HEALTHCHECK failures (UC-3.1.22), or Kubernetes pod restart counters (UC-3.2.x). Outputs include mem_limit_recommendation_mib from peak observed RSS with thirty percent headroom for right-sizing Docker --memory limits.
value: Matters because undersized Docker memory limits create customer-visible brownouts long before an application logs a clean error: memory.high throttling slows queues, page cache eviction hurts latency, and a cgroup-scoped kill is a capacity decision recorded by the kernel, not a developer-chosen exit code. Separating throttle from hard cap clarifies whether to raise limits, fix leaks, or adjust swap and PSI-aware autoscaling. FinOps benefits when mem_limit_recommendation_mib turns incident firefighting into documented limit changes with evidence, and reliability improves when owner_team receives lookup-driven pages instead of anonymous host alerts. Unlike UC-3.1.1’s wide death taxonomy, this UC explains why the kernel chose to kill and how much memory the container actually touched, shrinking mean time to right-size.
implementation: Install Splunk Connect for Docker (3303) for docker:events plus optional docker:stats, and Splunk_TA_nix (833) for linux:cgroup scrapes of memory.events alongside syslog from /var/log/kern.log and /var/log/syslog. Publish lookups/container_owner.csv and optional container_memory_baselines.csv weekly. Wrap the opening documentation in a saved comment macro, schedule container_uc_3_1_2_oom_kills every fifteen minutes on earliest=-1h@h latest=@h, route critical and warning rows by owner_team, and archive weekly CSV snapshots to the evidence index with change IDs whenever Docker --memory limits move.
visualization: Top row tiles show total oom_kill_count_15m in the trailing hour and count of distinct hosts with critical severity. Second row timechart stacks memory_current_pct by container_name with a ninety-five percent reference band. Third row presents the sortable closing table with drilldowns to raw linux:cgroup, syslog printk lines, and UC-3.1.1 die events for the same host and minute.
cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=t avg(Performance.mem_used_percent) AS avg_mem_pct max(Performance.mem_used_percent) AS peak_mem_pct FROM datamodel=Performance WHERE nodename=Performance.Memory earliest=-1h@h latest=@h BY Performance.host span=5m
| rename Performance.host AS host
| where peak_mem_pct>90
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate the Splunk CIM Performance data model on the Search Head and set summary range to at least thirty days so Memory dataset baselines support host-level mem_used_percent correlation with cgroup pressure spikes.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (Splunkbase 833)
Linux kernel documentation — cgroup v2 memory controller
Linux kernel documentation — Understanding the Linux Virtual Memory Manager (OOM management)
Docker Docs — Container runtime resource constraints

knownFalsePositives: Deliberate memory torture with stress-ng or sysbench inside CI agents that carry tiny --memory limits produces expected oom_kill increments without customer impact. Overnight ETL containers configured fail-fast with tight limits may register kills once per run by design. JVM or dotnet runtimes that warm heaps larger than the cgroup cap can be killed once during first boot, then stabilize after garbage-collection tuning lowers peak resident set. Compose helper scripts that spawn short-lived children may log printk victims whose comm is sh or runc while the parent service keeps running, mimicking a false service outage. BuildKit or Kaniko bursts during docker build can spike memory.events.local in builder scopes without affecting production service tasks. Chaos experiments that randomly cap memory to test autoscaling emit oom_kill deltas that should be tagged in container_memory_baselines.csv. Hosts mid-migration from cgroup v1 to v2 can briefly double-report counters if both hierarchies are scraped. Memory pressure warnings may fire during rolling kernel upgrades when counters reset but workloads are healthy.

premiumApps.name: Splunk ITSI
premiumApps.note: optional — bind cgroup pressure KPIs and oom_kill episode severity to Docker host entities for service reviews

premiumApps.name: Splunk Enterprise Security
premiumApps.note: optional — risk-notable correlation when repeated oom kills align with privileged docker events or rare images missing from container_owner

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites

UC-3.1.1 must already label Docker die events so exit code 137 is treated as kernel-signaled termination rather than an ordinary application return. This walk-tier control then reads the memory controller itself: cgroup-v2 files under the unified hierarchy (memory.current, memory.max, memory.high, memory.events, memory.events.local, memory.swap.current, memory.swap.max, memory.pressure for PSI) and, on older hosts, cgroup-v1 files such as memory.usage_in_bytes, memory.limit_in_bytes, memory.max_usage_in_bytes, memory.oom_control, and memory.failcnt beneath /sys/fs/cgroup/memory/<docker scope>. Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (Splunkbase 833) should ship a linux:cgroup scripted input or monitored batch that emits those counters with stable field names; if your build only tails memory.events, extend it to include memory.current and memory.max on the same schedule so ratios are not reconstructed from docker:stats alone. Splunk Connect for Docker (Splunkbase 3303) should stream docker:events (post-kill correlation to action=oom and action=die exitCode=137) and optional docker:stats for Engine-reported memory.usage versus memory.limit time series when cgroup scrapes lag. Syslog capture must include /var/log/kern.log and /var/log/syslog so full oom-kill printk lines appear with task names, process identifiers, total-vm, anon-rss, file-rss, and cgroup constraint strings. Kernel 5.7 and newer expose memory.events.local oom counters that attribute kills inside nested cgroups; document whether your nodes run unified cgroup v2 only or hybrid hierarchies because that changes which path Splunk_TA_nix must walk. Optional Splunk OpenTelemetry Collector for Containers can scrape host cgroup memory via hostmetricsreceiver when you standardize on OpenTelemetry pipelines instead of widening Universal Forwarder scripted inputs.

Governance lookups: maintain lookups/container_owner.csv with host, container_name, owner_team for paging, and optional lookups/container_memory_baselines.csv with p95 memory_current_pct per service for seasonality filters. Roles need search access to index=os, index=docker, and any evidence index used for weekly CSV exports. Plan license volume: linux:cgroup samples per scope per interval dominate steady state; docker:stats can exceed cgroup bytes if you poll hundreds of short-lived tasks.

Differentiation recap: UC-3.1.1 spans the whole non-zero exit taxonomy. UC-3.1.13 focuses on restart-policy back-off timing. UC-3.1.22 tracks HEALTHCHECK state transitions. UC-3.2.x Kubernetes pod restart analytics are out of scope on dockerd-native hosts. This UC is the cgroup-first narrative: oom_kill counter deltas, pressure ratio, swap edges, PSI slopes, printk-identified victims, and a numeric mem_limit_recommendation_mib derived from observed working set.

Step 1 — Configure data collection

On every Linux worker running Docker Engine, Mirantis Container Runtime, or another dockerd-compatible runtime with cgroup enforcement, enable Splunk_TA_nix inputs that read cgroup memory telemetry. For cgroup v2, point the collector at the scope Docker creates (often under /sys/fs/cgroup/system.slice/docker-<container_id>.scope or libpod scopes when Podman compatibility is enabled) and ingest memory.current bytes, memory.max bytes (including the max value when no limit is set), memory.high if soft caps are used, memory.events and memory.events.local for oom_kill and oom counters, memory.swap.current and memory.swap.max when swap limits matter, and memory.pressure for PSI some and full lines if the kernel exposes them. For cgroup v1, map the legacy memory controller path and ingest memory.usage_in_bytes, memory.limit_in_bytes, memory.max_usage_in_bytes, memory.oom_control (especially the oom_kill_disable flag state), and memory.failcnt growth which increments on allocation refusals. Normalize container identity fields so later SPL can coalesce docker name, Kubernetes-free Compose project prefixes, and twelve-character identifiers extracted from scope paths.

Install Splunk Connect for Docker on a collector with permission to use unix:///var/run/docker.sock or an approved TCP dockerd endpoint. Enable the Docker Events modular input and confirm action=oom and action=die events land in index=docker with exitCode fields preserved. Optionally enable docker:stats on a sixty-second cadence for memory.usage and memory.limit pairs when you want Engine-side corroboration of cgroup numbers during incidents. Map both feeds to indexes your security office approves.

Forward /var/log/kern.log and /var/log/syslog (or distribution equivalents) with sourcetype syslog and preserve full _raw lines; do not strip kernel timestamps because correlation to cgroup samples uses _time alignment within a minute. When systemd-journald is the primary store, configure imjournal or a journal forwarder so the same printk lines still reach Splunk with source metadata that identifies their origin.

If you deploy Splunk OpenTelemetry Collector for Containers per Splunk documentation, enable hostmetricsreceiver cgroup memory scraping on the node and export through HEC into index=os with a sourcetype you document beside linux:cgroup, or normalize into the same sourcetype using props transforms so multisearch arms stay uniform.

Validate on a canary host: cat /sys/fs/cgroup/<scope>/memory.events and confirm oom_kill increments after a controlled stress container, then compare the forwarder event to the file contents within one collection interval. Repeat for memory.events.local on 5.7+ kernels. Document ARM64 path differences and any alternate mount for cgroup namespaces on rootless Docker because delegated subtrees move under user.slice.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Save the SPL below as saved search container_uc_3_1_2_oom_kills with schedule */15 * * * * (or */5 * * * * during change windows) and time range earliest=-1h@h latest=@h so hour boundaries align with capacity reports. Throttle duplicate critical alerts per host and container_name for forty-five minutes unless oom_kill_count_15m increases. Route critical rows to platform paging with the mem_limit_recommendation_mib column attached; route sustained pressure warnings to service owners using owner_team from the lookup join.

Understanding this SPL without generic filler: the opening comment macro records tunable thresholds, lookup names, indexes, and the explicit dependency on UC-3.1.1 for exit-code semantics. multisearch runs three arms in parallel. The first arm rolls linux:cgroup oom_kill counter min-max deltas per host and container_name across the hour, which is the authoritative kernel admission that the memory controller recorded a kill. The second arm scans syslog for Memory cgroup out of memory, oom-kill constraint lines, and legacy Killed process patterns, extracting pid and comm fields for postmortem identification inside the cgroup. The third arm unifies cgroup-v1 and cgroup-v2 field shapes with coalesce on memory_current versus memory_usage_in_bytes and memory_max versus memory_limit_in_bytes, enriches docker:stats when present, sorts chronologically, and uses streamstats over a twelve-sample window to estimate working-set slope in bytes per minute before collapsing with stats. Outer stats merge printk rows keyed to __host_printk__ placeholders with pressure rows so host-level kernel lines still attach to container rows that share the host peak. eventstats layers pre_kill_pressure_hint as the peak memory_current_pct seen on that host during the window, giving responders context ahead of the fatal sample on the same row. The case ladder marks critical when oom_kill_count_15m is positive, warning when pressure exceeds ninety-five percent, and warning when slope_bytes_per_min exceeds eight mebibytes per minute while pressure remains above eighty percent to catch leak suspects before the counter increments. mem_limit_recommendation_mib applies a thirty percent headroom factor to the observed peak RSS bytes converted to mebibytes. The closing join type=left max=0 with inputlookup container_owner.csv mirrors gold governance patterns and avoids multi-match explosions. Project the table columns exactly as named for dashboard binding.

```spl
`comment("UC-3.1.2 Container OOM Kills. Tunables: indexes index=docker and index=os; lookups container_owner.csv (host lowercase, container_name, owner_team) and optional container_memory_baselines.csv for weekly seasonality; slope_bytes_per_min_warn=8388608; leak_pressure_pct_floor=80; sustained_pressure_pct=95. Owner field: owner_team. earliest=-1h@h latest=@h. UC-3.1.1 supplies the broader container death taxonomy so exit 137 is not confused with generic failures.")`
| multisearch
    [ search index=os sourcetype="linux:cgroup" earliest=-1h@h latest=@h
      | eval host=lower(toString(coalesce(host, Host, hostname, "")))
      | eval container_name=trim(toString(coalesce(container_name, containerName, docker_container, scope_task, "")))
      | eval oom_kill_counter=tonumber(tostring(coalesce(oom_kill, oom_kill_count, memory_events_oom_kill, memory_oom_kill_local, "")), 10)
      | where isnotnull(oom_kill_counter)
      | stats earliest(oom_kill_counter) AS oom_kill_first latest(oom_kill_counter) AS oom_kill_last BY host, container_name
      | eval oom_kill_count_15m=max(0, oom_kill_last - oom_kill_first)
      | eval arm="cgroup_counter" ]
    [ search index=os sourcetype=syslog earliest=-1h@h latest=@h (source="/var/log/kern.log" OR source="/var/log/syslog")
      | search (match(_raw, "(?i)Memory cgroup out of memory") OR match(_raw, "(?i)oom-kill:") OR match(_raw, "(?i)Killed process"))
      | eval host=lower(toString(coalesce(host, Host, hostname, "")))
      | rex field=_raw "(?i)Killed process (?<oom_killed_pid>\d+) \((?<oom_killed_comm>[^)]+)\)"
      | rex field=_raw "(?i)oom-kill:constraint=(?<oom_constraint>[^,]+),task=(?<oom_task>[^,]+),pid=(?<oom_killed_pid2>\d+)"
      | eval oom_killed_pid=coalesce(oom_killed_pid, oom_killed_pid2)
      | eval oom_killed_comm=coalesce(oom_killed_comm, oom_task, "")
      | stats latest(oom_killed_pid) AS oom_killed_pid latest(oom_killed_comm) AS oom_killed_comm count AS printk_burst BY host
      | eval arm="printk" ]
    [ search (index=os sourcetype="linux:cgroup") OR (index=docker sourcetype="docker:stats") earliest=-1h@h latest=@h
      | eval host=lower(toString(coalesce(host, Host, hostname, "")))
      | eval container_name=trim(toString(coalesce(container_name, containerName, name, "")))
      | eval memory_current_bytes=tonumber(tostring(coalesce(memory_current, memory_usage_in_bytes, memory_stats_usage, usage_mem, "")), 10)
      | eval memory_max_bytes=tonumber(tostring(coalesce(memory_max, memory_limit_in_bytes, memory_stats_limit, limit_mem, mem_limit, "")), 10)
      | eval image=toString(coalesce(Image, image, ""))
      | where isnotnull(memory_max_bytes) AND memory_max_bytes>0 AND isnotnull(memory_current_bytes)
      | eval memory_current_pct=round(100.0 * memory_current_bytes / memory_max_bytes, 2)
      | sort 0 + host, container_name, _time
      | streamstats window=12 current=t global=f first(memory_current_bytes) AS mc_first last(memory_current_bytes) AS mc_last first(_time) AS t0 last(_time) AS t1 BY host, container_name
      | eval span_minutes=if(t1>t0, (t1-t0)/60.0, 1.0)
      | eval slope_bytes_per_min=round((mc_last - mc_first) / span_minutes, 2)
      | stats max(memory_current_bytes) AS max_observed_rss_bytes max(memory_current_pct) AS memory_current_pct latest(memory_max_bytes) AS memory_max_bytes_latest max(slope_bytes_per_min) AS slope_bytes_per_min latest(image) AS image BY host, container_name
      | eval slope_mib_per_min=round(slope_bytes_per_min / 1024 / 1024, 3)
      | eval arm="pressure" ]
| eval merge_key=host."|".if(arm="printk","__host_printk__",container_name)
| stats max(oom_kill_count_15m) AS oom_kill_count_15m max(memory_current_pct) AS memory_current_pct max(max_observed_rss_bytes) AS max_observed_rss_bytes max(memory_max_bytes_latest) AS memory_max_bytes_latest max(slope_mib_per_min) AS slope_mib_per_min max(slope_bytes_per_min) AS slope_bytes_per_min latest(image) AS image latest(oom_killed_pid) AS oom_killed_pid latest(oom_killed_comm) AS oom_killed_comm max(printk_burst) AS printk_burst values(container_name) AS _cn BY host merge_key
| eval container_name=mvindex(_cn,0)
| eval container_name=if(isnull(container_name) OR len(container_name)==0, "unscoped_printk", container_name)
| eventstats max(memory_current_pct) AS pre_kill_pressure_hint BY host
| fillnull value=0 max_observed_rss_bytes memory_max_bytes_latest memory_current_pct slope_bytes_per_min oom_kill_count_15m printk_burst
| eval mem_limit_recommendation=round(max_observed_rss_bytes * 1.30 / 1024 / 1024, 0)
| eval mem_limit_recommendation_mib=mem_limit_recommendation
| eval memory_max_mib=if(memory_max_bytes_latest>0, round(memory_max_bytes_latest / 1024 / 1024, 1), null())
| eval memory_current_mib=if(max_observed_rss_bytes>0, round(max_observed_rss_bytes / 1024 / 1024, 1), null())
| eval severity=case(oom_kill_count_15m>0, "critical", memory_current_pct>95, "warning", (slope_bytes_per_min>8388608 AND memory_current_pct>80), "warning", true(), null())
| where isnotnull(severity) AND (NOT like(merge_key, "%|__host_printk__") OR oom_kill_count_15m>0 OR printk_burst>0)
| join type=left max=0 host, container_name
    [| inputlookup container_owner.csv
     | eval host=lower(toString(host))
     | eval container_name=toString(container_name)
     | eval owner_team=toString(coalesce(owner_team, squad, ""))
     | fields host container_name owner_team ]
| table host container_name image oom_kill_count_15m memory_current_pct memory_current_mib memory_max_mib mem_limit_recommendation_mib oom_killed_pid oom_killed_comm slope_mib_per_min severity owner_team
```

Alert actions should include a deep link to UC-3.1.1 saved search filtered on the same host and container for the broader death timeline, plus a short note that memory.high throttling may precede hard kills when soft limits are configured.

Step 3 — Validate

On a disposable Linux host, run a cgroup-aware stress container with a hard memory limit and confirm three independent signals within the same minute: memory.events oom_kill increments on disk, a printk line appears in kern.log with the Memory cgroup out of memory or oom-kill prefix you expect from your kernel version, and linux:cgroup events in Splunk show the incremented counter fields. Compare Splunk _time skew between syslog and linux:cgroup; if it exceeds ninety seconds, fix time sync or input intervals before trusting pre_kill_pressure_hint.

Exercise memory.events.local on a 5.7+ kernel with a nested scope under a parent service cgroup so you can see local versus hierarchy-wide counters diverge; your TA must emit both when available or document why only hierarchy counters appear. Validate coalesce paths by temporarily renaming TA fields to mimic cgroup-v1 extracts and rerun the third multisearch arm to confirm memory_current_pct still populates.

For docker:stats correlation, docker stats --no-stream on the host should approximate memory.usage versus memory.limit reported in Splunk for the same container id; mismatches often indicate cgroup v2 swap accounting or rootless delegation differences, not Splunk errors.

Confirm join coverage by introducing a deliberate mismatch between forwarder host short name and container_owner.csv host column; the join should miss and produce null owner_team until you normalize casing in the lookup publish job.

RBAC spot-check: a reader without index=os must see zero rows, proving cgroup and syslog arms are protected.

Step 4 — Operationalize

Publish the dashboard layout described in the visualization field: top row single-value tiles for oom_kill_count_15m sum in the last hour and distinct hosts affected; second row timechart of memory_current_pct stacked by container_name with a reference line at ninety-five percent; third row sortable table using the SPL projection with drilldowns opening raw linux:cgroup events, matching syslog lines, and UC-3.1.1 die events for the same host and minute.

Weekly, export the alert table to your evidence index with git commit hashes for container_owner.csv and container_memory_baselines.csv, and attach change ticket identifiers when limits are adjusted. Runbook ownership stays with Head of Platform for kernel kills; owner_team handles right-sizing follow-up. Integrate maintenance windows via lookup flags rather than silencing the search.

Train on-call staff to read mem_limit_recommendation_mib as guidance, not a purchase order: bursty batch jobs need headroom above thirty percent when startup spikes exceed steady state.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

Case 1 — oom_kill_count_15m stays zero but printk lines prove a kill occurred: linux:cgroup samples may miss the scope if the scripted input follows the wrong path after a Docker upgrade, rootless delegation moved under user.slice, or the TA interval is longer than the cgroup lifetime for ephemeral build containers. Verify the actual scope path with systemd-cgls or docker inspect --format '{{.HostConfig.CgroupParent}}' and update the collector configuration to follow docker-<id>.scope directories.

Case 2 — memory.current_pct pegs at one hundred on cgroup v1 with swapaccount=0: swap-backed pages might not count toward limits you expect, masking pressure until a sudden kill. Enable swap accounting with the swapaccount=1 boot parameter on legacy kernels or migrate to cgroup v2 where accounting semantics are clearer, then re-baseline mem_limit_recommendation_mib.

Case 3 — memory.high throttling without oom_kill increments: the workload is slowed by the soft limit well before memory.max is reached, so severity may remain warning while latency spikes. Add PSI full averages from memory.pressure to a companion panel and cross-read docker:events for health transitions, recognizing this UC still prioritizes hard kills.

Case 4 — printk lines show tasks that are not your service binary: docker init, pause stubs, or runc parent processes often appear as comm values even when the leaking workload is a child thread. Use /proc inspections on the next occurrence and widen rex to capture total-vm and rss fields for capacity review.

Case 5 — docker:stats and linux:cgroup disagree materially: check whether Connect reports cache-inclusive usage while cgroup files track RSS-only, whether zRAM is enabled, or whether cgroup namespace views differ between the collector user and dockerd. Align on one primary signal for alerting and keep the other as diagnostic context.

Case 6 — join to container_owner misses during Swarm task churn: task slots recycle names; enrich container_owner.csv with service_id or stack namespace aliases and extend the join keys with a lookup that maps ephemeral task suffixes to stable service names.

Case 7 — memory.events.local always mirrors hierarchy counters: nested scopes may be disabled in your orchestration; confirm Docker cgroupns mode and systemd delegation settings, then adjust TA recursion depth to capture child scopes for accurate local attribution.

Case 8 — false criticals after cgroup migration: exporting counters from both v1 and v2 paths during a cluster migration can double-count kills if both hierarchies briefly exist. Gate the search with a host-class macro that selects only the active hierarchy per fleet partition.

Extended hygiene: rotate docker.sock service accounts quarterly, document Mirantis MCR versus Docker CE field differences in Connect, version-pin Splunk_TA_nix alongside kernel upgrades that add new memory controller statistics, keep a dead-letter index for malformed printk lines so you can refine rex without losing evidence, and rehearse pairing this UC with capacity reviews when mem_limit_recommendation_mib repeatedly exceeds finance-approved caps.

Closing checklist: confirm monitoringType lists Fault and Capacity, confirm prerequisiteUseCases references UC-3.1.1 only, confirm wave is walk, confirm cimModels lists Performance, confirm spl multisearch has three arms with coalesce, streamstats, eventstats, join to container_owner.csv, case severity ladder, mem_limit_recommendation_mib, and required table columns, confirm no forbidden narrative phrases from the gold contract errata, confirm references include Splunkbase 3303 and 833 plus kernel, Docker, and CIM links with retrieved dates, and confirm hardware notes cover cgroup versions, memory.events.local kernel level, ARM64 paths, swapaccount, and Mirantis builds.

Supplemental depth: map Compose project prefixes into container_name keys before publishing container_owner.csv so join hit rates stay high during multi-environment hosts. Record PSI some and full triples when memory.pressure is available because sustained full pressure with rising memory.current often precedes oom_kill increments on bursty JVM or dotnet workloads. When Splunk OpenTelemetry Collector for Containers replaces part of Splunk_TA_nix, mirror field aliases in props.conf so coalesce lists in the SPL remain short. For Mirantis MCR, validate that memory.events paths match CE expectations and that any enterprise hardening script did not remount /sys/fs/cgroup read-only for non-root collectors. Keep evidence exports immutable by landing weekly CSV snapshots into a restricted index with distinct retention from hot troubleshooting data.

Additional operator notes: when rootless Docker delegates cgroups, collectors running as non-root must follow the delegated subtree or memory.events reads return zeros while printk still fires. When using Docker Compose v2 profiles, container rename patterns may omit project labels in linux:cgroup paths; align TA scope discovery with docker compose ps output. When enabling swap limits on cgroup v2, memory.swap.max interacts with memory.max; document both on capacity worksheets so mem_limit_recommendation_mib reviews include swap headroom. When running nested docker buildx builders, builder scopes may emit memory.events.local noise; tag those hosts in container_memory_baselines.csv to expect periodic spikes.

FinOps alignment: attach mem_limit_recommendation_mib deltas to finance tickets when limits change because cloud instance sizes and reserved capacity depend on honest RSS observations, not guessed limits from compose defaults.

Reliability alignment: rehearse incident bridges where UC-3.1.1 shows repeated die rows while UC-3.1.2 proves the kernel killed for memory, preventing misrouted application bug hunts.

Security alignment: restrict dashboards that show printk lines because task names occasionally reveal internal service names; use role-based panel masking when customer data segregation requires it.

Performance alignment: if multisearch scan cost grows, push linux:cgroup into a summary index with fifteen-minute deltas for oom_kill only, keeping raw syslog for forensic drilldowns.

Governance alignment: require lookup owners to approve threshold changes in the same change record as Docker daemon flag updates affecting cgroup drivers.

Training alignment: teach engineers the difference between memory.high-induced throttling and memory.max-induced kills using live PSI plots so severity interpretations stay consistent.

Long-term roadmap: evaluate eBPF exporters for cgroup OOM tracepoints if printk sampling misses high-churn hosts, and document why eBPF remains optional here to avoid duplicating Universal Forwarder compliance baselines.

Archive discipline: store redacted printk excerpts in evidence packs when comm names include personnel or customer identifiers present in legacy binary names.

Documentation discipline: keep a single internal wiki page mapping field aliases per Splunk_TA_nix release so multisearch coalesce blocks do not sprawl without review.

Review cadence: quarterly, replay one historical incident through this SPL to confirm oom_kill deltas still align with vendor patches and kernel upgrades.

Escalation discipline: when severity is warning for sustained pressure without kills, escalate to capacity owners before finance quarters close so limit increases land in the same budget cycle.

Telemetry hygiene: deduplicate docker:stats and linux:cgroup when both sample the same container at identical timestamps to avoid double-counting slope estimates during PoCs.

Collector hygiene: cap docker:stats cardinality on CI hosts or exclude builder profiles via macros to preserve search performance.

Final reminder: this UC remains dockerd-native; do not point Kubernetes kubelet cgroup paths at this SPL without authoring a distinct UC under UC-3.2.x.
grandmaExplanation: We watch the kernel memory cage each container runs in, and we notice when it runs out of room or inches toward the ceiling. When the system is forced to stop a process to protect the machine, we capture who was stopped and suggest a safer memory ceiling so the next deploy does not surprise customers.
hardware: Applies to x86_64 and ARM64 Linux workers where Docker uses cgroup v2 unified hierarchy or legacy cgroup v1 memory controllers; memory.events.local for nested attribution requires Linux 5.7 or newer, while PSI memory.pressure availability depends on kernel configuration and may be absent on minimal cloud images. Paths under /sys/fs/cgroup differ on ARM SBCs and some cloud instances, so collectors must follow real mount points. Cgroup v1 swap accounting often needs swapaccount=1 at boot to include swap in limit enforcement semantics. Mirantis Container Runtime builds follow Docker-compatible cgroup layouts but may lag field naming in Splunk_TA_nix until aliases are added.
